title: SEGMENT 14 — Somatic vs germline pharmacogenomics: a tale of two tests

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Somatic vs germline pharmacogenomics: a tale of two tests
I want to introduce a critical distinction now that often confuses people coming into pharmacogenomics — and that confused the field itself for years.
There are actually two completely different things we call "pharmacogenomics," and they operate on different genomes, with different logistics, in different clinical contexts.
The first — and the one we've been discussing this entire lecture so far — is germline pharmacogenomics.
This is testing the patient's inherited DNA, the DNA they were born with, the DNA in every cell of their body.
CYP2D6, CYP2C9, TPMT, HLA-B\*5701 — these are all germline tests.
The result is stable across the patient's lifetime.
Test once, use forever.
The variants you're looking for are inherited from your parents and present from birth.
The second is somatic pharmacogenomics, which is mostly the territory of oncology.
Cancer cells have their own genome — actually, a corrupted derivative of the patient's germline genome, with accumulated mutations specific to the tumor.
Many modern cancer drugs are targeted to specific somatic mutations in the tumor.
To know whether the drug will work, you don't test the patient — you test the tumor.
Let me give you a couple of examples.
BRAF V600E and vemurafenib.
In melanoma, about half of all tumors carry a specific mutation in the BRAF gene — the substitution of glutamate for valine at amino acid position 600.
This V600E mutation produces a hyperactive BRAF protein that drives uncontrolled cell growth.
The drug vemurafenib is a small molecule that specifically inhibits BRAF V600E — not normal BRAF, just the mutant form.
If you have melanoma and your tumor carries BRAF V600E, vemurafenib can produce dramatic, often life-saving, responses.
If your tumor doesn't carry V600E — if you have wild-type BRAF in your tumor — vemurafenib won't work, and may actually make things worse by paradoxically activating wild-type BRAF.
So before prescribing vemurafenib, you sequence the tumor for V600E.
Tumor genotyping.
Somatic pharmacogenomics.
EGFR exon 19 deletions and erlotinib.
In non-small-cell lung cancer, a subset of tumors carry mutations in the EGFR gene — most commonly small in-frame deletions in exon 19, or a specific point mutation in exon 21.
These mutations make the EGFR receptor hyperactive and drive tumor growth.
Drugs like erlotinib, gefitinib, and the next-generation osimertinib specifically inhibit EGFR with these activating mutations.
If your lung cancer carries the right EGFR mutation, these drugs work spectacularly well — multi-year responses, in some cases.
If your tumor has wild-type EGFR, they barely work.
So before prescribing, you sequence the tumor.
Somatic pharmacogenomics.
The list of these somatic-genotype-to-drug-choice pairings has expanded enormously over the past 15 years.
ALK rearrangements in lung cancer and crizotinib.
HER2 amplification in breast and gastric cancer and trastuzumab.
KRAS G12C and sotorasib.
BCR-ABL fusion in CML and imatinib.
PIK3CA mutations and alpelisib.
The entire field of "precision oncology" is, fundamentally, somatic pharmacogenomics scaled up.
The critical distinction is workflow.
Germline pharmacogenomics is patient-centered — you test the patient once, the result is permanent, it informs many future drug decisions.
Somatic pharmacogenomics is tumor-centered — you test the tumor each time, because each new tumor (or each new biopsy of a progressing tumor) may have a different mutational profile.
The infrastructure is different.
The labs are different.
The turnaround time, the cost structure, the clinical context — all different.
Some practical consequences.
Germline pharmacogenomics testing is moving toward upfront, panel-based, preemptive deployment integrated into primary care.

Somatic pharmacogenomics testing is delivered through pathology, often through "tumor sequencing panels" that cover dozens or hundreds of cancer-relevant genes at a time, typically ordered when a cancer is first diagnosed or when it progresses.
Both fields use the same word — pharmacogenomics — and both depend on the same fundamental insight, that genetic variation in genes that interact with the drug determines whether the drug will work.
But operationally they're parallel disciplines.
When you read about pharmacogenomics in the news, it helps to ask: is this a germline test, looking at the patient's inherited DNA, or a somatic test, looking at the tumor?
The clinical workflow, and the implications, will differ significantly.
For the rest of this lecture I'm going to focus mostly on germline pharmacogenomics, because that's the field with the broadest implications across all of medicine.

But just know that somatic pharmacogenomics is a huge and rapidly-growing parallel discipline, and that for cancer patients in particular, both kinds of test may matter — somatic to choose the targeted drug, germline to dose the chemotherapy backbone.
